Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores a Federated Learning (FL)-based strategy for beam selection in LEO satellite constellations using realistic data. A Graph Neural Network (GNN) is shown to outperform a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) in prediction accuracy and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to ensure efficient and intelligent beam management in LEO NTNs, especially due to the dynamic propagation conditions and the growing need for lightweight, distributed solutions.

Method: Federated Learning (FL) is applied with Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and Graph Neural Network (GNN) models trained on realistic channel and beamforming data across distributed satellite learners.

Result: The GNN model outperforms the MLP model in terms of both prediction accuracy and stability, particularly at low elevation angles, suggesting superior beam management.

Conclusion: GNN-based approaches are highly effective for intelligent and lightweight beam selection in future Low Earth Orbit Non-Terrestrial Networks.

Abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs) require efficient beam management under dynamic propagation conditions. This work investigates Federated Learning (FL)-based beam selection in LEO satellite constellations, where orbital planes operate as distributed learners through the utilization of High-Altitude Platform Stations (HAPS). Two models, a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and a Graph Neural Network (GNN), are evaluated using realistic channel and beamforming data. Results demonstrate that GNN surpasses MLP in beam prediction accuracy and stability, particularly at low elevation angles, enabling lightweight and intelligent beam management for future NTN deployments.

</details>


### [306] [The Discrete Charm of the MLP: Binary Routing of Continuous Signals in Transformer Feed-Forward Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10985)
*Peter Balogh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLP layers in transformer language models route signals using binary neuron activations—tokens need nonlinear processing based on these activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the architecture and functionality of MLP layers in transformer language models and how they route signals for processing.

Method: Identify routing mechanisms through binary neuron activation analysis, cross-layer development studies, and causal validation experiments in GPT-2 Small.

Result: Found consensus and exception handlers in neurons with functional routing and validated binary routing efficacy with minimal information loss compared to continuous features.

Conclusion: Binary routing explains processing structure in transformers and complements the piecewise-affine characterization with token-based routing along data manifolds.

Abstract: We show that MLP layers in transformer language models perform binary routing of continuous signals: the decision of whether a token needs nonlinear processing is well-captured by binary neuron activations, even though the signals being routed are continuous. In GPT-2 Small (124M parameters), we find that specific neurons implement a consensus architecture -- seven "default-ON" neurons and one exception handler (N2123 in Layer 11) that are 93-98% mutually exclusive -- creating a binary routing switch. A cross-layer analysis reveals a developmental arc: early layers (L1-3) use single gateway neurons to route exceptions without consensus quorums; middle layers (L4-6) show diffuse processing with neither gateway nor consensus; and late layers (L7-11) crystallize full consensus/exception architectures with increasing quorum size (1 to 3 to 7 consensus neurons). Causal validation confirms the routing is functional: removing the MLP at consensus breakdown costs 43.3% perplexity, while at full consensus removing it costs only 10.1% -- exceeding a 4x difference. Comparing binary vs. continuous features for the routing decision confirms that binarization loses essentially no information (79.2% vs. 78.8% accuracy), while continuous activations carry additional magnitude information (R^2 = 0.36 vs. 0.22). This binary routing structure explains why smooth polynomial approximation fails: cross-validated polynomial fits (degrees 2-7) never exceed R^2 = 0.06 for highly nonlinear layers. We propose that the well-established piecewise-affine characterization of deep networks can be complemented by a routing characterization: along the natural data manifold, the piecewise boundaries implement binary decisions about which tokens need nonlinear processing, routing continuous signals through qualitatively different computational paths.

</details>


### [307] [MCMC Informed Neural Emulators for Uncertainty Quantification in Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10987)
*Heikki Haario,Zhi-Song Liu,Martin Simon,Hendrik Weichel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper decouples uncertainty quantification from neural network architecture by integrating model-parameter distributions in training via MCMC methods, resulting in reduced computation times while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of exhaustive training times and unphysical parameter evaluations caused by directly sampling or screening model parameters, and to enable computationally efficient uncertainty quantification.

Method: The paper introduces a technique where the model-parameter distribution is included as input to network training via Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), allowing uncertainty quantification to be decoupled from the neural network architecture.

Result: The proposed method achieves the same level of uncertainty quantification as physical models but with significantly reduced computational effort. Demonstrations include a quantile emulator for prediction and an autoencoder-based ODE network emulator.

Conclusion: The approach is flexible and agnostic to network choice, providing accurate and efficient surrogate modeling while transparently linking performance to distribution mismatch through mathematical analysis.

Abstract: Neural networks are a commonly used approach to replace physical models with computationally cheap surrogates. Parametric uncertainty quantification can be included in training, assuming that an accurate prior distribution of the model parameters is available. Here we study the common opposite situation, where direct screening or random sampling of model parameters leads to exhaustive training times and evaluations at unphysical parameter values. Our solution is to decouple uncertainty quantification from network architecture. Instead of sampling network weights, we introduce the model-parameter distribution as an input to network training via Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). In this way, the surrogate achieves the same uncertainty quantification as the underlying physical model, but with substantially reduced computation time. The approach is fully agnostic with respect to the neural network choice. In our examples, we present a quantile emulator for prediction and a novel autoencoder-based ODE network emulator that can flexibly estimate different trajectory paths corresponding to different ODE model parameters. Moreover, we present a mathematical analysis that provides a transparent way to relate potential performance loss to measurable distribution mismatch.

</details>


### [308] [Factorized Neural Implicit DMD for Parametric Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10995)
*Siyuan Chen,Zhecheng Wang,Yixin Chen,Yue Chang,Peter Yichen Chen,Eitan Grinspun,Jonathan Panuelos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel model-free approach for modeling physical systems dynamics using a physics-coded neural field to parameterize the Koopman operator's spectral decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Handling high-dimensional and nonlinear dynamics for long-term predictions is computationally challenging with traditional solvers; a model-free, data-driven approach is needed for practical real-time applications.

Method: The authors design a physics-coded neural field to represent the Koopman operator's spectral decomposition, separating spatial modes and temporal evolution to support stable rollouts and spectral analysis.

Result: The model effectively predicts complex spatiotemporal dynamics and provides insights into eigenvalues, stability, and behavior across parameter spaces.

Conclusion: This method enables accurate and scalable modeling of physical systems, offering interpretable insights into system dynamics and significantly improving prediction capabilities over traditional methods.

Abstract: A data-driven, model-free approach to modeling the temporal evolution of physical systems mitigates the need for explicit knowledge of the governing equations. Even when physical priors such as partial differential equations are available, such systems often reside in high-dimensional state spaces and exhibit nonlinear dynamics, making traditional numerical solvers computationally expensive and ill-suited for real-time analysis and control. Consider the problem of learning a parametric flow of a dynamical system: with an initial field and a set of physical parameters, we aim to predict the system's evolution over time in a way that supports long-horizon rollouts, generalization to unseen parameters, and spectral analysis.
  We propose a physics-coded neural field parameterization of the Koopman operator's spectral decomposition. Unlike a physics-constrained neural field, which fits a single solution surface, and neural operators, which directly approximate the solution operator at fixed time horizons, our model learns a factorized flow operator that decouples spatial modes and temporal evolution. This structure exposes underlying eigenvalues, modes, and stability of the underlying physical process to enable stable long-term rollouts, interpolation across parameter spaces, and spectral analysis. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method on a range of dynamics problems, showcasing its ability to accurately predict complex spatiotemporal phenomena while providing insights into the system's dynamic behavior.

</details>


### [309] [Leech Lattice Vector Quantization for Efficient LLM Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11021)
*Tycho F. A. van der Ouderaa,Mart van Baalen,Paul Whatmough,Markus Nagel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Leech Lattice Vector Quantization (LLVQ) for compressing large language models, achieving superior efficiency over prior methods using high-dimensional lattice techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of scalar quantization in LLMs by leveraging vector quantization while addressing practical implementation challenges without costly lookup systems.

Method: The paper introduces LLVQ, which extends Leech lattice-based approaches using algorithms supporting efficient indexing, angular searches, and parallel dequantization, removing the need for explicit codebook storage.

Result: LLVQ provides state-of-the-art performance in LLM quantization, surpassing recent methods like Quip#, QTIP, and PVQ.

Conclusion: High-dimensional lattices, such as the Leech lattice, provide impactful tools for scalable, efficient model compression, setting new benchmarks in LLM quantization.

Abstract: Scalar quantization of large language models (LLMs) is fundamentally limited by information-theoretic bounds. While vector quantization (VQ) overcomes these limits by encoding blocks of parameters jointly, practical implementations must avoid the need for expensive lookup mechanisms or other explicit codebook storage. Lattice approaches address this through highly structured and dense packing. This paper explores the Leech lattice, which, with its optimal sphere packing and kissing configurations at 24 dimensions, is the highest dimensional lattice known with such optimal properties. To make the Leech lattice usable for LLM quantization, we extend an existing search algorithm based on the extended Golay code construction, to i) support indexing, enabling conversion to and from bitstrings without materializing the codebook, ii) allow angular search over union of Leech lattice shells, iii) propose fully-parallelisable dequantization kernel. Together this yields a practical algorithm, namely Leech Lattice Vector Quantization (LLVQ). LLVQ delivers state-of-the-art LLM quantization performance, outperforming recent methods such as Quip\#, QTIP, and PVQ. These results highlight the importance of high-dimensional lattices for scalable, theoretically grounded model compression.

</details>


### [310] [Neural Field Thermal Tomography: A Differentiable Physics Framework for Non-Destructive Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11045)
*Tao Zhong,Yixun Hu,Dongzhe Zheng,Aditya Sood,Christine Allen-Blanchette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeFTY is a framework for reconstructing 3D material properties from surface temperature measurements using neural fields and differentiable physics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for thermography struggle with lateral diffusion effects and challenges in transient diffusion scenarios, hindering accurate 3D reconstructions.

Method: NeFTY parameterizes 3D diffusivity fields using continuous neural fields optimized through a differentiable physics solver, enforcing thermodynamic laws rigorously.

Result: NeFTY demonstrates superior accuracy in identifying subsurface defects in synthetic datasets compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: NeFTY effectively addresses spectral bias and ill-posedness in inverse heat conduction, offering robust tools for high-resolution tomography at arbitrary scales.

Abstract: We propose Neural Field Thermal Tomography (NeFTY), a differentiable physics framework for the quantitative 3D reconstruction of material properties from transient surface temperature measurements. While traditional thermography relies on pixel-wise 1D approximations that neglect lateral diffusion, and soft-constrained Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) often fail in transient diffusion scenarios due to gradient stiffness, NeFTY parameterizes the 3D diffusivity field as a continuous neural field optimized through a rigorous numerical solver. By leveraging a differentiable physics solver, our approach enforces thermodynamic laws as hard constraints while maintaining the memory efficiency required for high-resolution 3D tomography. Our discretize-then-optimize paradigm effectively mitigates the spectral bias and ill-posedness inherent in inverse heat conduction, enabling the recovery of subsurface defects at arbitrary scales. Experimental validation on synthetic data demonstrates that NeFTY significantly improves the accuracy of subsurface defect localization over baselines. Additional details at https://cab-lab-princeton.github.io/nefty/

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [311] [Intrinsic Numerical Robustness and Fault Tolerance in a Neuromorphic Algorithm for Scientific Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10246)
*Bradley H. Theilman,James B. Aimone*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates a neuromorphic algorithm's robustness to structural perturbations, highlighting its fault tolerance and tunable accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether neuromorphic computing, inspired by the brain, can inherently tolerate faults like neuron loss or spikes dropped.

Method: Analyzing a spiking neuromorphic algorithm for solving partial differential equations, testing its response to neuron ablation and spike dropping.

Result: The algorithm tolerates up to 32% neuron loss and 90% dropped spikes without significant accuracy degradation, and the robustness is adjustable through hyperparameters.

Conclusion: Neuromorphic computing models inspired by brain function can achieve high levels of intrinsic fault tolerance, akin to the robustness observed in biological systems.

Abstract: The potential for neuromorphic computing to provide intrinsic fault tolerance has long been speculated, but the brain's robustness in neuromorphic applications has yet to be demonstrated. Here, we show that a previously described, natively spiking neuromorphic algorithm for solving partial differential equations is intrinsically tolerant to structural perturbations in the form of ablated neurons and dropped spikes. The tolerance band for these perturbations is large: we find that as many as 32 percent of the neurons and up to 90 percent of the spikes may be entirely dropped before a significant degradation in the accuracy results. Furthermore, this robustness is tunable through structural hyperparameters. This work demonstrates that the specific brain-like inspiration behind the algorithm contributes to a significant degree of robustness expected from brain-like neuromorphic algorithms.

</details>


### [312] [An Event-Driven E-Skin System with Dynamic Binary Scanning and real time SNN Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10537)
*Gaishan Li,Zhengnan Fu,Anubhab Tripathi,Junyi Yang,Arindam Basu*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper introduces a high-speed electronic skin system integrating sensing and neuromorphic computing using a sparse event-driven strategy for tactile data acquisition and processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an efficient solution for robotic perception and human-computer interaction by reducing data acquisition overhead and improving processing efficiency in electronic skin systems.

Method: The system uses a 16x16 piezoresistive tactile array with an event-based binary scan search strategy and processes sparse tactile data with a convolutional spiking neural network (Conv-SNN) implemented on an FPGA.

Result: Event-driven scanning reduces scan counts (12.8x), compresses data (38.2x), increases dynamic range (28.4x), and achieves 99% sparsity. Conv-SNN achieves significant efficiency gains with 92.11% accuracy for handwritten digit recognition.

Conclusion: The integrated pipeline enables efficient robotic perception and human-computer interaction with significant reductions in data processing requirements, demonstrating potential for real-time applications in tactile sensing.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel hardware system for high-speed, event-sparse sampling-based electronic skin (e-skin)that integrates sensing and neuromorphic computing. The system is built around a 16x16 piezoresistive tactile array with front end and introduces a event-based binary scan search strategy to classify the digits. This event-driven strategy achieves a 12.8x reduction in scan counts, a 38.2x data compression rate and a 28.4x equivalent dynamic range, a 99% data sparsity, drastically reducing the data acquisition overhead. The resulting sparse data stream is processed by a multi-layer convolutional spiking neural network (Conv-SNN) implemented on an FPGA, which requires only 65% of the computation and 15.6% of the weight storage relative to a CNN. Despite these significant efficiency gains, the system maintains a high classification accuracy of 92.11% for real-time handwritten digit recognition. Furthermore, a real neuromorphic tactile dataset using Address Event Representation (AER) is constructed. This work demonstrates a fully integrated, event-driven pipeline from analog sensing to neuromorphic classification, offering an efficient solution for robotic perception and human-computer interaction.

</details>


<div id='cs.PF'></div>

# cs.PF [[Back]](#toc)

### [313] [RAGPerf: An End-to-End Benchmarking Framework for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10765)
*Shaobo Li,Yirui Zhou,Yuan Xu,Kevin Chen,Daniel Waddington,Swaminathan Sundararaman,Hubertus Franke,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.PF

TL;DR: The paper introduces RAGPerf, a framework for benchmarking AI RAG workflows with modular characterization, configurability, and varied dataset support, showcasing negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for detailed profiling and performance analysis of RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines by offering a modular, customizable, and performance-aware benchmarking framework.

Method: RAGPerf decouples RAG workflow into modular components (e.g., embedding, indexing) and allows configuration of their parameters. It supports diverse datasets, embedding models, vector databases, and LLMs. Real-world benchmarking is facilitated through a workload generator and automated collection of performance and accuracy metrics.

Result: RAGPerf demonstrates extensive support for diverse configurations of RAG pipelines while collecting key performance and accuracy metrics. It showcases minimal performance overhead in comprehensive evaluations.

Conclusion: RAGPerf facilitates fine-grained and customizable benchmarking for RAG pipelines, enabling better analysis and optimization. Its open-source availability supports broader adoption and further research.

Abstract: We present the design and implementation of a RAG-based AI system benchmarking (RAGPerf) framework for characterizing the system behaviors of RAG pipelines. To facilitate detailed profiling and fine-grained performance analysis, RAGPerf decouples the RAG workflow into several modular components - embedding, indexing, retrieval, reranking, and generation. RAGPerf offers the flexibility for users to configure the core parameters of each component and examine their impact on the end-to-end query performance and quality. RAGPerf has a workload generator to model real-world scenarios by supporting diverse datasets (e.g., text, pdf, code, and audio), different retrieval and update ratios, and query distributions. RAGPerf also supports different embedding models, major vector databases such as LanceDB, Milvus, Qdrant, Chroma, and Elasticsearch, as well as different LLMs for content generation. It automates the collection of performance metrics (i.e., end-to-end query throughput, host/GPU memory footprint, and CPU/GPU utilization) and accuracy metrics (i.e., context recall, query accuracy, and factual consistency). We demonstrate the capabilities of RAGPerf through a comprehensive set of experiments and open source its codebase at GitHub. Our evaluation shows that RAGPerf incurs negligible performance overhead.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [314] [Fully Symbolic Analysis of Loop Locality: Using Imaginary Reuse to Infer Real Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10196)
*Yifan Zhu,Yekai Pan,Chen Ding,Yanghui Wu*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a symbolic theory of locality, leveraging compiler techniques for affine loop nests and achieving highly accurate data movement predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the precision and generality of cache-performance prediction for scientific computations and tensor operations.

Method: They developed a symbolic theory of locality and compiler analysis, enabling the derivation of locality polynomials for affine loop nests.

Result: The method achieves 99.6% accuracy in data movement prediction across various tests; deriving locality polynomials takes 41 seconds on average, with near-instant cache miss count predictions.

Conclusion: The approach provides a highly efficient and precise means to predict cache performance, surpassing traditional empirical rules in accuracy and generality.

Abstract: This paper presents a new theory of locality and its compiler support. The theory is fully symbolic and derives locality as polynomials, and the compiler analysis supports affine loop nests. They derive cache-performance scaling in quadratic and reciprocal expressions and are more general and precise than empirical scaling rules.
  Evaluated on a benchmark suite of 41 scientific kernels and tensor operations, the compiler requires an average of 41 seconds to derive the locality polynomials. After derivation, predicting the cache miss count for any given input size and cache configuration takes less than a millisecond. Across all tests--with and without loop fusion--the accuracy in the data movement prediction is 99.6\%, compared to simulated set-associative L1 data cache.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [315] [OmniGuide: Universal Guidance Fields for Enhancing Generalist Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10052)
*Yunzhou Song,Long Le,Yong-Hyun Park,Jie Wang,Junyao Shi,Lingjie Liu,Jiatao Gu,Eric Eaton,Dinesh Jayaraman,Kostas Daniilidis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces OMNIGUIDE, a flexible framework to enhance vision-language-action (VLA) models on challenging tasks by leveraging various sources of guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limited performance of existing VLA models on complex tasks involving spatial or semantic understanding and precise manipulation.

Method: OMNIGUIDE uses differentiable energy functions with task-specific attractors and repellers in 3D space to integrate diverse sources of guidance (e.g., 3D foundation models, semantic VLMs).

Result: OMNIGUIDE improves the performance of state-of-the-art VLA models (e.g., $π_{0.5}$, GR00T N1.6), increasing success and safety rates across tasks.

Conclusion: OMNIGUIDE successfully combines multiple sources of guidance in a unified framework, surpassing previous methods in improving VLA policies for complex tasks.

Abstract: Vision-language-action(VLA) models have shown great promise as generalist policies for a large range of relatively simple tasks. However, they demonstrate limited performance on more complex tasks, such as those requiring complex spatial or semantic understanding, manipulation in clutter, or precise manipulation. We propose OMNIGUIDE, a flexible framework that improves VLA performance on such tasks by leveraging arbitrary sources of guidance, such as 3D foundation models, semantic-reasoning VLMs, and human pose models. We show how many kinds of guidance can be naturally expressed as differentiable energy functions with task-specific attractors and repellers located in 3D space, that influence the sampling of VLA actions. In this way, OMNIGUIDE enables guidance sources with complementary task-relevant strengths to improve a VLA model's performance on challenging tasks. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments, across diverse sources of guidance, demonstrate that OMNIGUIDE enhances the performance of state-of-the-art generalist policies (e.g., $π_{0.5}$, GR00T N1.6) significantly across success and safety rates. Critically, our unified framework matches or surpasses the performance of prior methods designed to incorporate specific sources of guidance into VLA policies. Project Page: $\href{https://omniguide.github.io/}{this \; url}$

</details>


### [316] [Model-Free Co-Optimization of Manufacturable Sensor Layouts and Deformation Proprioception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10059)
*Yingjun Tian,Guoxin Fang,Aoran Lyu,Xilong Wang,Zikang Shi,Yuhu Guo,Weiming Wang,Charlie C. L. Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a computational pipeline to optimize flexible sensor layouts and network parameters for predicting large freeform deformations, improving accuracy without physical models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practices rely on heuristics or trial-and-error for sensor layout in soft robotics and wearable devices, which impacts prediction accuracy.

Method: The authors developed a data-driven, model-free pipeline that co-optimizes sensor layout and prediction network parameters using differentiable loss functions accounting for accuracy and manufacturability.

Result: Numerical and physical experiments demonstrate significantly improved prediction accuracy and practical feasibility compared to unoptimized sensor layouts.

Conclusion: The co-optimization approach ensures better performance and applicability across robotic sensing tasks, validating its effectiveness for diverse systems.

Abstract: Flexible sensors are increasingly employed in soft robotics and wearable devices to provide proprioception of freeform deformations.Although supervised learning can train shape predictors from sensor signals, prediction accuracy strongly depends on sensor layout, which is typically determined heuristically or through trial-and-error. This work introduces a model-free, data-driven computational pipeline that jointly optimizes the number, length, and placement of flexible length-measurement sensors together with the parameters of a shape prediction network for large freeform deformations. Unlike model-based approaches, the proposed method relies solely on datasets of deformed shapes, without requiring physical simulation models, and is therefore broadly applicable to diverse robotic sensing tasks. The pipeline incorporates differentiable loss functions that account for both prediction accuracy and manufacturability constraints. By co-optimizing sensor layouts and network parameters, the method significantly improves deformation prediction accuracy over unoptimized layouts while ensuring practical feasibility. The effectiveness and generality of the approach are validated through numerical and physical experiments on multiple soft robotic and wearable systems.

</details>


### [317] [Decision-Aware Uncertainty Evaluation of Vision-Language Model-Based Early Action Anticipation for Human-Robot Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10061)
*Zhaoda Du,Michael Bowman,Qiaojie Zheng,Xiaoli Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper evaluates uncertainty and reliability of vision-language models in action recognition for human-robot interaction under partial observations, introducing new protocols and metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve reliability in predicting human actions for robots in shared workspaces, especially under ambiguous, partial observations and egocentric views.

Method: Developed a temporal-prefix evaluation protocol and calibration metrics to systematically assess vision-language models' reliability in action recognition.

Result: Identified reliability gaps, miscalibration patterns, and failure modes in vision-language models under partial observations.

Conclusion: The study highlights the importance of reliable uncertainty estimation in using vision-language models for confidence-gated human-robot interactions.

Abstract: Robots in shared workspaces must interpret human actions from partial, ambiguous observations, where overconfident early predictions can lead to unsafe or disruptive interaction. This challenge is amplified in egocentric views, where viewpoint changes and occlusions increase perceptual noise and ambiguity. As a result, downstream human-robot interaction modules require not only an action hypothesis but also a trustworthy estimate of confidence under partial observation. Recent vision-language model-based approaches have been proposed for short-term action recognition due to their open-vocabulary and context-aware reasoning, but their uncertainty reliability in the temporal-prefix regime is largely uncharacterized. We present the first systematic evaluation of uncertainty in vision-language model-based short-term action recognition for human-robot interaction. We introduce a temporal-prefix evaluation protocol and metrics for calibration and selective prediction. We also characterize miscalibration patterns and failure modes under partial observations. Our study provides the missing reliability evidence needed to use vision-language model predictions in confidence-gated human-robot interaction modules.

</details>


### [318] [AR-VLA: True Autoregressive Action Expert for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10126)
*Yutong Hu,Jan-Nico Zaech,Nikolay Nikolov,Yuanqi Yao,Sombit Dey,Giuliano Albanese,Renaud Detry,Luc Van Gool,Danda Paudel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes an autoregressive Action Expert to generate context-aware robotic actions using long-term memory, improving over reactive action models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing Vision-Language-Action models and diffusion policies that lack persistent context tracking and generate reactive actions.

Method: Develop an autoregressive Action Expert that uses refreshable vision-language prefixes, long-lived memory, and a re-anchoring mechanism for synchronization between fast kinematic control and slow perception reasoning.

Result: The approach exhibits better history awareness, smoother trajectories, and comparable or improved task success rates in robotic manipulation tasks compared to reactive models.

Conclusion: The method contributes a scalable framework for generating spatio-temporally consistent robotic actions, establishing a reliable base for training advanced robotic policies.

Abstract: We propose a standalone autoregressive (AR) Action Expert that generates actions as a continuous causal sequence while conditioning on refreshable vision-language prefixes. In contrast to existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and diffusion policies that reset temporal context with each new observation and predict actions reactively, our Action Expert maintains its own history through a long-lived memory and is inherently context-aware. This structure addresses the frequency mismatch between fast control and slow reasoning, enabling efficient independent pretraining of kinematic syntax and modular integration with heavy perception backbones, naturally ensuring spatio-temporally consistent action generation across frames. To synchronize these asynchronous hybrid V-L-A modalities, we utilize a re-anchoring mechanism that mathematically accounts for perception staleness during both training and inference. Experiments on simulated and real-robot manipulation tasks demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively replace traditional chunk-based action heads for both specialist and generalist policies. AR-VLA exhibits superior history awareness and substantially smoother action trajectories while maintaining or exceeding the task success rates of state-of-the-art reactive VLAs. Overall, our work introduces a scalable, context-aware action generation schema that provides a robust structural foundation for training effective robotic policies.

</details>


### [319] [Cross-Hand Latent Representation for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10158)
*Guangqi Jiang,Yutong Liang,Jianglong Ye,Jia-Yang Huang,Changwei Jing,Rocky Duan,Pieter Abbeel,Xiaolong Wang,Xueyan Zou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes XL-VLA, a vision-language-action framework with a shared latent action space to enable dexterous robot manipulation across diverse robotic hands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of scalable cross-embodiment learning for dexterous manipulation, as collecting data for diverse robotic hands becomes increasingly costly and impractical.

Method: Introduce XL-VLA, a framework with a unified latent action space that is embodiment-invariant and integrates into vision-language-action architectures.

Result: Experimental results show that XL-VLA outperforms traditional VLA models that utilize raw joint spaces, facilitating better cross-embodiment learning and data reuse.

Conclusion: XL-VLA is effective for scalable learning in dexterous robotic systems and enables efficient reuse of data across different robotic hands.

Abstract: Dexterous manipulation is essential for real-world robot autonomy, mirroring the central role of human hand coordination in daily activity. Humans rely on rich multimodal perception--vision, sound, and language-guided intent--to perform dexterous actions, motivating vision-based, language-conditioned manipulation systems for robots. However, training reliable vision-language-action (VLA) models for dexterous manipulation requires large-scale demonstrations across many robotic hands. In addition, as new dexterous embodiments appear rapidly, collecting data for each becomes costly and impractical, creating a need for scalable cross-embodiment learning. We introduce XL-VLA, a vision-language-action framework integrated with a unified latent action space shared across diverse dexterous hands. This embodiment-invariant latent space is directly pluggable into standard VLA architectures, enabling seamless cross-embodiment training and efficient reuse of both existing and newly collected data. Experimental results demonstrate that XL-VLA consistently outperforms baseline VLA models operating in raw joint spaces, establishing it as an effective solution for scalable cross-embodiment dexterous manipulation.

</details>


### [320] [Dance2Hesitate: A Multi-Modal Dataset of Dancer-Taught Hesitancy for Understandable Robot Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10166)
*Srikrishna Bangalore Raghu,Anna Soukhovei,Divya Sai Sindhuja Vankineni,Alexandra Bacula,Alessandro Roncone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a dataset focusing on hesitant motions in human-robot collaboration, capturing multi-modal and context-specific behaviors, including robot and dancer demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In human-robot collaboration, observing hesitant motions impacts coordination and safety judgments, but creating generalized hesitant motions across contexts is challenging.

Method: The authors developed and open-sourced a multi-modal dataset based on robot and human dancer demonstrations of hesitant motions, categorized across different levels in specific contexts.

Result: The dataset includes 70 unique whole-body trajectories, 84 upper-limb trajectories, and 66 kinesthetic teaching trajectories, annotated with three hesitancy levels.

Conclusion: This dataset facilitates reproducible benchmarking for studying hesitant motions in robots and humans, enhancing understanding in human-robot collaboration scenarios.

Abstract: In human-robot collaboration, a robot's expression of hesitancy is a critical factor that shapes human coordination strategies, attention allocation, and safety-related judgments. However, designing hesitant robot motion that generalizes is challenging because the observer's inference is highly dependent on embodiment and context. To address these challenges, we introduce and open-source a multi-modal, dancer-generated dataset of hesitant motion where we focus on specific context-embodiment pairs (i.e., manipulator/human upper-limb approaching a Jenga Tower, and anthropomorphic whole body motion in free space). The dataset includes (i) kinesthetic teaching demonstrations on a Franka Emika Panda reaching from a fixed start configuration to a fixed target (a Jenga tower) with three graded hesitancy levels (slight, significant, extreme) and (ii) synchronized RGB-D motion capture of dancers performing the same reaching behavior using their upper limb across three hesitancy levels, plus full human body sequences for extreme hesitancy. We further provide documentation to enable reproducible benchmarking across robot and human modalities. Across all dancers, we obtained 70 unique whole-body trajectories, 84 upper limb trajectories spanning over the three hesitancy levels, and 66 kinesthetic teaching trajectories spanning over the three hesitancy levels. The dataset can be accessed here: https://brsrikrishna.github.io/Dance2Hesitate/.

</details>


### [321] [Characterizing Healthy & Post-Stroke Neuromotor Behavior During 6D Upper-Limb Isometric Gaming: Implications for Design of End-Effector Rehabilitation Robot Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10173)
*Ajay Anand,Gabriel Parra,Chad A. Berghoff,Laura A. Hallock*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper analyzes user neuromotor behaviors using a rehabilitation robot and evaluates how system design influences performance and pathology detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve robot-mediated rehabilitation by understanding the interaction between user behavior, robot intervention, and gaming interfaces.

Method: The authors used the OpenRobotRehab 1.0 dataset to analyze neuromotor behaviors through isometric trajectory tasks with 13 healthy and 2 post-stroke users. They employed force dynamics assessment, interface design analysis, and HMM-based classification using sEMG signals.

Result: Pathological features were detectable in end-effector force dynamics with differences in force production and error ($p=0.05$). Task design strongly influenced user behavior, and HMM-based classifications highlighted differences between healthy and post-stroke neuromotor patterns.

Conclusion: The findings point to the need for adaptive robot designs to accommodate diverse user neuromotor profiles, promoting healthy motor strategies effectively.

Abstract: Successful robot-mediated rehabilitation requires designing games and robot interventions that promote healthy motor practice. However, the interplay between a given user's neuromotor behavior, the gaming interface, and the physical robot makes designing system elements -- and even characterizing what behaviors are "healthy" or pathological -- challenging. We leverage our OpenRobotRehab 1.0 open access data set to assess the characteristics of 13 healthy and 2 post-stroke users' force output, muscle activations, and game performance while executing isometric trajectory tracking tasks using an end-effector rehabilitation robot. We present an assessment of how subtle aspects of interface design impact user behavior; an analysis of how pathological neuromotor behaviors are reflected in end-effector force dynamics; and a novel hidden Markov model (HMM)-based neuromotor behavior classification method based on surface electromyography (sEMG) signals during cyclic motions. We demonstrate that task specification (including which axes are constrained and how users interpret tracking instructions) shapes user behavior; that pathology-related features are detectable in 6D end-effector force data during isometric task execution (with significant differences between healthy and post-stroke profiles in force error and average force production at $p=0.05$); and that healthy neuromotor strategies are heterogeneous and inherently difficult to characterize. We also show that our HMM-based models discriminate healthy and post-stroke neuromotor dynamics where synergy-based decompositions reflect no such differentiation. Lastly, we discuss these results' implications for the design of adaptive end-effector rehabilitation robots capable of promoting healthier movement strategies across diverse user populations.

</details>


### [322] [Autonomous Search for Sparsely Distributed Visual Phenomena through Environmental Context Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10174)
*Eric Chen,Travis Manderson,Nare Karapetyan,Peter Edmunds,Nicholas Roy,Yogesh Girdhar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for AUVs to efficiently locate specific coral species by leveraging visual environmental context for searching in sparse distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of limited battery life and sparse distribution of coral species in underwater surveys, requiring efficient AUV navigation.

Method: Using patch-level DINOv2 embeddings for one-shot detection to identify both target coral species and their environmental context as directional guidance.

Result: Validation on real imagery shows this method achieves sampling of 75% of targets in half the time compared to exhaustive surveys, outperforming strategies focused solely on target detection.

Conclusion: Environmental context-based adaptive planning significantly improves surveying efficiency for AUVs in sparse coral reef distributions.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) are increasingly used to survey coral reefs, yet efficiently locating specific coral species of interest remains difficult: target species are often sparsely distributed across the reef, and an AUV with limited battery life cannot afford to search everywhere. When detections of the target itself are too sparse to provide directional guidance, the robot benefits from an additional signal to decide where to look next. We propose using the visual environmental context -- the habitat features that tend to co-occur with a target species -- as that signal. Because context features are spatially denser and often vary more smoothly than target detections, we hypothesize that a reward function targeted at broader environmental context will enable adaptive planners to make better decisions on where to go next, even in regions where no target has yet been observed. Starting from a single labeled image, our method uses patch-level DINOv2 embeddings to perform one-shot detections of both the target species and its surrounding context online. We validate our approach using real imagery collected by an AUV at two reef sites in St. John, U.S. Virgin Islands, simulating the robot's motion offline. Our results demonstrate that one-shot detection combined with adaptive context modeling enables efficient autonomous surveying, sampling up to 75$\%$ of the target in roughly half the time required by exhaustive coverage when the target is sparsely distributed, and outperforming search strategies that only use target detections.

</details>


### [323] [Octopus-inspired Distributed Control for Soft Robotic Arms: A Graph Neural Network-Based Attention Policy with Environmental Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10198)
*Linxin Hou,Qirui Wu,Zhihang Qin,Yongxin Guo,Cecilia Laschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SoftGM is a robot control architecture inspired by octopus arms, designed for segmented soft robots in contact-rich environments. It uses cooperative agents and graph-based interaction modeling for efficient obstacle handling and robust performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance the control of segmented soft robotic arms in environments with obstacles, especially where the geometry of obstacles isn’t globally known.

Method: It uses a graph-based architecture where arm sections are treated as cooperative agents applying a two-stage graph attention message passing scheme under a Centralised Training Decentralised Execution paradigm.

Result: SoftGM was tested in environments of increasing complexity, outperforming six baseline methods in challenging scenarios like navigating through a wall-with-hole environment.

Conclusion: SoftGM demonstrates resilient coordination with selective contact-relevant information handling, maintaining performance despite disturbances or failures, indicating its potential for robust robotic applications.

Abstract: This paper proposes SoftGM, an octopus-inspired distributed control architecture for segmented soft robotic arms that learn to reach targets in contact-rich environments using online obstacle discovery without relying on global obstacle geometry. SoftGM formulates each arm section as a cooperative agent and represents the arm-environment interaction as a graph. SoftGM uses a two-stage graph attention message passing scheme following a Centralised Training Decentralised Execution (CTDE) paradigm with a centralised critic and decentralised actor. We evaluate SoftGM in a Cosserat-rod simulator (PyElastica) across three tasks that increase the complexity of the environment: obstacle-free, structured obstacles, and a wall-with-hole scenario. Compared with six widely used MARL baselines (IDDPG, IPPO, ISAC, MADDPG, MAPPO, MASAC) under identical information content and training conditions, SoftGM matches strong CTDE methods in simpler settings and achieves the best performance in the wall-with-hole task. Robustness tests with observation noise, single-section actuation failure, and transient disturbances show that SoftGM preserves success while keeping control effort bounded, indicating resilient coordination driven by selective contact-relevant information routing.

</details>


### [324] [Perceptive Hierarchical-Task MPC for Sequential Mobile Manipulation in Unstructured Semi-Static Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10227)
*Xintong Du,Jingxing Qian,Siqi Zhou,Angela P. Schoellig*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel framework for sequential mobile manipulation in environments that change over time, emphasizing adaptability and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sequential mobile manipulation tasks demand adaptability to dynamic environments over extended periods, which current motion planners lack.

Method: The paper proposes a perceptive hierarchical-task model predictive control (HTMPC) framework utilizing Bayesian inference for real-time environment modeling and lexicographic optimization for task execution.

Result: The proposed HTMPC method demonstrates higher efficiency and reactivity in completing sequential tasks compared to baseline methods in both simulations and real-robot experiments.

Conclusion: The approach successfully addresses environment changes during sequential tasks without relying on static maps or external infrastructure, enhancing robotic adaptability and performance.

Abstract: As compared to typical mobile manipulation tasks, sequential mobile manipulation poses a unique challenge -- as the robot operates over extended periods, successful task completion is not solely dependent on consistent motion generation but also on the robot's awareness and adaptivity to changes in the operating environment. While existing motion planners can generate whole-body trajectories to complete sequential tasks, they typically assume that the environment remains static and rely on precomputed maps. This assumption often breaks down during long-term operations, where semi-static changes such as object removal, introduction, or shifts are common. In this work, we propose a novel perceptive hierarchical-task model predictive control (HTMPC) framework for efficient sequential mobile manipulation in unstructured, changing environments. To tackle the challenge, we leverage a Bayesian inference framework to explicitly model object-level changes and thereby maintain a temporally accurate representation of the 3D environment; this up-to-date representation is embedded in a lexicographic optimization framework to enable efficient execution of sequential tasks. We validate our perceptive HTMPC approach through both simulated and real-robot experiments. In contrast to baseline methods, our approach systematically accounts for moved and phantom obstacles, successfully completing sequential tasks with higher efficiency and reactivity, without relying on prior maps or external infrastructure.

</details>


### [325] [Hierarchical Task Model Predictive Control for Sequential Mobile Manipulation Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10232)
*Xintong Du,Siqi Zhou,Angela P. Schoellig*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Hierarchical-Task Model Predictive Control framework for mobile manipulators, achieving significant improvements in task execution efficiency and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a seamless decision-making algorithm for mobile manipulators that integrates with high-level task planners and efficiently executes sequential tasks.

Method: The authors propose a Hierarchical-Task Model Predictive Control framework based on nonlinear lexicographic optimization for improved task performance and reactivity in sequential task execution.

Result: The proposed framework improved hierarchical trajectory tracking performance by 42% and execution time by 2.3 times faster in task space compared to existing methods, validated through experiments on a 9 degrees of freedom manipulator.

Conclusion: The framework effectively utilizes the robot's redundancy, offering enhanced reactivity, efficiency, and shorter task completion paths, thereby advancing mobile manipulator task execution.

Abstract: Mobile manipulators are envisioned to serve more complex roles in people's everyday lives. With recent breakthroughs in large language models, task planners have become better at translating human verbal instructions into a sequence of tasks. However, there is still a need for a decision-making algorithm that can seamlessly interface with the high-level task planner to carry out the sequence of tasks efficiently. In this work, building on the idea of nonlinear lexicographic optimization, we propose a novel Hierarchical-Task Model Predictive Control framework that is able to complete sequential tasks with improved performance and reactivity by effectively leveraging the robot's redundancy. Compared to the state-of-the-art task-prioritized inverse kinematic control method, our approach has improved hierarchical trajectory tracking performance by 42% on average when facing task changes, robot singularity and reference variations. Compared to a typical single-task architecture, our proposed hierarchical task control architecture enables the robot to traverse a shorter path in task space and achieves an execution time 2.3 times faster when executing a sequence of delivery tasks. We demonstrated the results with real-world experiments on a 9 degrees of freedom mobile manipulator.

</details>


### [326] [COHORT: Hybrid RL for Collaborative Large DNN Inference on Multi-Robot Systems Under Real-Time Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10436)
*Mohammad Saeid Anwar,Anuradha Ravi,Indrajeet Ghosh,Gaurav Shinde,Carl Busart,Nirmalya Roy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: COHORT is a collaborative framework for large DNN inference in multi-robot systems, optimizing resource use and performance under constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of deploying computationally demanding large DNNs in resource-constrained, autonomous, multi-robot systems for tasks like disaster response.

Method: COHORT uses a hybrid offline-online RL approach: (a) Offline RL via Advantage-Weighted Regression trained on auction-based task allocation data, and (b) Online adaptation using Multi-Agent PPO initialized from the offline policy to distribute DNN inference in real-time.

Result: COHORT reduces battery usage by 15.4%, improves GPU utilization by 51.67%, and meets task deadlines 2.55x more often compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: COHORT effectively optimizes collaborative DNN inference and task execution for multi-robot systems, improving energy efficiency and scalability while maintaining performance constraints.

Abstract: Large deep neural networks (DNNs), especially transformer-based and multimodal architectures, are computationally demanding and challenging to deploy on resource-constrained edge platforms like field robots. These challenges intensify in mission-critical scenarios (e.g., disaster response), where robots must collaborate under tight constraints on bandwidth, latency, and battery life, often without infrastructure or server support. To address these limitations, we present COHORT, a collaborative DNN inference and task-execution framework for multi-robot systems built on the Robotic Operating System (ROS). COHORT employs a hybrid offline-online reinforcement learning (RL) strategy to dynamically schedule and distribute DNN module execution across robots. Our key contributions are threefold: (a) Offline RL policy learning combined with Advantage-Weighted Regression (AWR), trained on auction-based task allocation data from heterogeneous DNN workloads across distributed robots, (b) Online policy adaptation via Multi-Agent PPO (MAPPO), initialized from the offline policy and fine-tuned in real time, and (c) comprehensive evaluation of COHORT on vision-language model (VLM) inference tasks such as CLIP and SAM, analyzing scalability with increasing robot/workload and robustness under . We benchmark COHORT against genetic algorithms and multiple RL baselines. Experimental results demonstrate that COHORT reduces battery consumption by 15.4% and increases GPU utilization by 51.67%, while satisfying frame-rate and deadline constraints 2.55 times of the time.

</details>


### [327] [Degeneracy-Resilient Teach and Repeat for Geometrically Challenging Environments Using FMCW Lidar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10248)
*Katya M. Papais,Wenda Zhao,Timothy D. Barfoot*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a degeneracy-resilient topometric navigation system using FMCW lidar for autonomous path repetition in GPS-denied environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable autonomous navigation in GPS-denied, sparsely structured environments, where existing iterative closest point (ICP)-based systems fail due to ill-conditioning.

Method: The paper introduces a system using FMCW lidar with Doppler velocity-based odometry and degeneracy-aware scan-to-map localization for geometry-independent motion estimation and uncertainty handling.

Result: Closed-loop field tests in three different environments showed reliable autonomous navigation, including in a challenging flat airport field where typical ICP approaches failed.

Conclusion: The proposed navigation system proves robust and effective in degenerate, GPS-denied environments, offering significant improvement over state-of-the-art ICP-based systems.

Abstract: Teach and Repeat (T&R) topometric navigation enables robots to autonomously repeat previously traversed paths without relying on GPS, making it well suited for operations in GPS-denied environments such as underground mines and lunar navigation. State-of-the-art T&R systems typically rely on iterative closest point (ICP)-based estimation; however, in geometrically degenerate environments with sparsely structured terrain, ICP often becomes ill-conditioned, resulting in degraded localization and unreliable navigation performance. To address this challenge, we present a degeneracy-resilient Frequency-Modulated Continuous-Wave (FMCW) lidar T&R navigation system consisting of Doppler velocity-based odometry and degeneracy-aware scan-to-map localization. Leveraging FMCW lidar, which provides per-point radial velocity measurements via the Doppler effect, we extend a geometry-independent, correspondence-free motion estimation to include principled pose uncertainty estimation that remains stable in degenerate environments. We further propose a degeneracy-aware localization method that incorporates per-point curvature for improved data association, and unifies translational and rotational scales to enable consistent degeneracy detection. Closed-loop field experiments across three environments with varying structural richness demonstrate that the proposed system reliably completes autonomous navigation, including in a challenging flat airport test field where a conventional ICP-based system fails.

</details>


### [328] [From Prior to Pro: Efficient Skill Mastery via Distribution Contractive RL Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10263)
*Zhanyi Sun,Shuran Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DICE-RL refines pretrained generative robot policies using reinforcement learning to boost performance in manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enable robots to master complex manipulation skills efficiently by refining pretrained generative policies.

Method: The approach involves pretraining using diffusion or flow-based policies, then finetuning with a residual off-policy RL framework combining behavior regularization and value-guided action selection.

Result: Extensive experiments show strong performance improvements, stability, and sample efficiency in long-horizon tasks from high-dimensional pixel inputs.

Conclusion: DICE-RL successfully amplifies high-success behaviors and achieves mastery over manipulation skills in both simulation and real-world settings.

Abstract: We introduce Distribution Contractive Reinforcement Learning (DICE-RL), a framework that uses reinforcement learning (RL) as a "distribution contraction" operator to refine pretrained generative robot policies. DICE-RL turns a pretrained behavior prior into a high-performing "pro" policy by amplifying high-success behaviors from online feedback. We pretrain a diffusion- or flow-based policy for broad behavioral coverage, then finetune it with a stable, sample-efficient residual off-policy RL framework that combines selective behavior regularization with value-guided action selection. Extensive experiments and analyses show that DICE-RL reliably improves performance with strong stability and sample efficiency. It enables mastery of complex long-horizon manipulation skills directly from high-dimensional pixel inputs, both in simulation and on a real robot. Project website: https://zhanyisun.github.io/dice.rl.2026/.

</details>


### [329] [Design of a Robot-Assisted Chemical Dialysis System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10264)
*Diane Jung,Caleb Escobedo,Noah Liska,Maitrey Gramopadhye,Daniel Szafir,Alessandro Roncone,Carson Bruns*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper discusses a robot-assisted system designed to improve dialysis procedures in experimental science by decreasing manual workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the bottleneck caused by labor-intensive procedures in experimental science and reduce the burden on scientists.

Method: A user-centered design approach was used, incorporating feedback from usability studies to refine the robot-assisted dialysis system.

Result: The system fulfills scientist needs for reducing laborious tasks in dialysis procedures, with potential applications in broader workflows.

Conclusion: Integration of the system is expected to lower scientist workloads during extended experiments and could pave the way for more automated systems in research, accelerating scientific discovery and reducing manual effort.

Abstract: Scientists perform diverse manual procedures that are tedious and laborious. Such procedures are considered a bottleneck for modern experimental science, as they consume time and increase burdens in fields including material science and medicine. We employ a user-centered approach to designing a robot-assisted system for dialysis, a common multi-day purification method used in polymer and protein synthesis. Through two usability studies, we obtain participant feedback and revise design requirements to develop the final system that satisfies scientists' needs and has the potential for applications in other experimental workflows. We anticipate that integration of this system into real synthesis procedures in a chemical wet lab will decrease workload on scientists during long experimental procedures and provide an effective approach to designing more systems that have the potential to accelerate scientific discovery and liberate scientists from tedious labor.

</details>


### [330] [Update-Free On-Policy Steering via Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10282)
*Maria Attarian,Ian Vyse,Claas Voelcker,Jasper Gerigk,Evgenii Opryshko,Anas Almasri,Sumeet Singh,Yilun Du,Igor Gilitschenski*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces UF-OPS, a lightweight method to improve robot manipulation by steering the base Behavior Cloning policy based on success likelihood predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavior Cloning policies often struggle with brittle performance and precise manipulation, motivating the need for an adaptable system to improve their success rates.

Method: UF-OPS utilizes verifier functions trained on policy rollout data to predict success likelihood, steering the policy toward more successful actions during execution. Importantly, base policy parameters remain unchanged.

Result: The UF-OPS method demonstrated a 49% improvement in success rate over the base Behavior Cloning policy in both simulation and real-life tasks.

Conclusion: UF-OPS successfully enhances robot manipulation performance by adapting execution dynamically, proving to be an effective and flexible addition to Behavior Cloning approaches.

Abstract: In recent years, Behavior Cloning (BC) has become one of the most prevalent methods for enabling robots to mimic human demonstrations. However, despite their successes, BC policies are often brittle and struggle with precise manipulation. To overcome these issues, we propose UF-OPS, an Update-Free On-Policy Steering method that enables the robot to predict the success likelihood of its actions and adapt its strategy at execution time. We accomplish this by training verifier functions using policy rollout data obtained during an initial evaluation of the policy. These verifiers are subsequently used to steer the base policy toward actions with a higher likelihood of success. Our method improves the performance of black-box diffusion policy, without changing the base parameters, making it light-weight and flexible. We present results from both simulation and real-world data and achieve an average 49% improvement in success rate over the base policy across 5 real tasks.

</details>


### [331] [SteadyTray: Learning Object Balancing Tasks in Humanoid Tray Transport via Residual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10306)
*Anlun Huang,Zhenyu Wu,Soofiyan Atar,Yuheng Zhi,Michael Yip*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReST-RL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning system for stabilizing payloads in bipedal humanoid locomotion, showcasing significant robustness and performance improvements in simulation and physical hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stabilizing unsecured payloads during bipedal locomotion in unstructured environments is challenging due to oscillations that compromise stability and transport effectiveness.

Method: ReST-RL decouples locomotion and payload stabilization with a robust locomotion policy and a residual module to cancel gait-induced perturbations. Evaluation is conducted using the SteadyTray benchmark.

Result: The residual module outperforms end-to-end learning methods in gait smoothness and orientation accuracy, achieving 96.9% success in velocity tracking and 74.5% robustness against external forces. It successfully generalizes to real-world usage on hardware.

Conclusion: The modular ReST-RL architecture achieves payload stabilization without compromising bipedal stability, demonstrating effective sim-to-real application and handling dynamic conditions in various environments.

Abstract: Stabilizing unsecured payloads against the inherent oscillations of dynamic bipedal locomotion remains a critical engineering bottleneck for humanoids in unstructured environments. To solve this, we introduce ReST-RL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning architecture that explicitly decouples locomotion from payload stabilization, evaluated via the SteadyTray benchmark. Rather than relying on monolithic end-to-end learning, our framework integrates a robust base locomotion policy with a dynamic residual module engineered to actively cancel gait-induced perturbations at the end-effector. This architectural separation ensures steady tray transport without degrading the underlying bipedal stability. In simulation, the residual design significantly outperforms end-to-end baselines in gait smoothness and orientation accuracy, achieving a 96.9% success rate in variable velocity tracking and 74.5% robustness against external force disturbances. Successfully deployed on the Unitree G1 humanoid hardware, this modular approach demonstrates highly reliable zero-shot sim-to-real generalization across various objects and external force disturbances.

</details>


### [332] [PC-Diffuser: Path-Consistent Capsule CBF Safety Filtering for Diffusion-Based Trajectory Planner](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10330)
*Eugene Ku,Yiwei Lyu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Autonomous driving planners face challenges in safety and generalization. PC-Diffuser is introduced to embed safety into the trajectory generation process while using diffusion planning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for autonomous driving planners to generalize beyond hand-crafted rules and avoid failures in out-of-distribution or rare scenarios.

Method: PC-Diffuser integrates a safety framework into the diffusion planning process, using capsule-distance barrier functions, kinematic bicycle models, and path-consistent filters.

Result: PC-Diffuser achieves iterative and context-aware safeguarding of trajectories, ensuring safety without distorting the generated path.

Conclusion: By embedding safety directly into trajectory generation, PC-Diffuser improves certifiability and robustness in autonomous driving scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous driving in complex traffic requires planners that generalize beyond hand-crafted rules, motivating data-driven approaches that learn behavior from expert demonstrations. Diffusion-based trajectory planners have recently shown strong closed-loop performance by iteratively denoising a full-horizon plan, but they remain difficult to certify and can fail catastrophically in rare or out-of-distribution scenarios. To address this challenge, we present PC-Diffuser, a safety augmentation framework that embeds a certifiable, path-consistent barrier-function structure directly into the denoising loop of diffusion planning. The key idea is to make safety an intrinsic part of trajectory generation rather than a post-hoc fix: we enforce forward invariance along the rollout while preserving the diffusion model's intended path geometry. Specifically, PC-Diffuser (i) evaluates collision risk using a capsule-distance barrier function that better reflects vehicle geometry and reduces unnecessary conservativeness, (ii) converts denoised waypoints into dynamically feasible motion under a kinematic bicycle model, and (iii) applies a path-consistent safety filter that eliminates residual constraint violations without geometric distortion, so the corrected plan remains close to the learned distribution. By injecting these safety-consistent corrections at every denoising step and feeding the refined trajectory back into the diffusion process, PC-Diffuser enables iterative, context-aware safeguarding instead of post-hoc repair...

</details>


### [333] [Adaptive Manipulation Potential and Haptic Estimation for Tool-Mediated Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10352)
*Lin Yang,Anirvan Dutta,Yuan Ji,Yanxin Zhou,Shilin Shan,Lv Chen,Etienne Burdet,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel closed-loop framework using a parameterized Equilibrium Manifold (EM) for tool-mediated manipulation with haptic sensing, validated in real-world screw-loosening trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to address the challenges in achieving human-level dexterity in tool-mediated manipulation, specifically visual occlusion and limitations in haptic sensing.

Method: The approach involves a unified EM representation, adaptive manipulation potential, haptic SLAM hybrid inference, and adaptive stiffness control to close the perception-action loop.

Result: Validated through simulation and over 260 real-world trials, the framework showed improved manipulation success and robustness while ensuring accurate tracking, outperforming fixed impedance baselines.

Conclusion: The introduced framework enhances manipulation capabilities by dynamically integrating haptic estimation, planning, and control, proving effective in handling contact-rich tool-mediated tasks.

Abstract: Achieving human-level dexterity in contact-rich, tool-mediated manipulation remains a significant challenge due to visual occlusion and the underdetermined nature of haptic sensing. This paper introduces a parameterized Equilibrium Manifold (EM) as a unified representation for tool-mediated interaction, and develops a closed-loop framework that integrates haptic estimation, online planning, and adaptive stiffness control. We establish a physical-geometric duality using an adaptive manipulation potential incorporating a differentiable contact model, which induces the manifold's geometric structure and ensures that complex physical interactions are encapsulated as continuous operations on the EM. Within this framework, we reformulate haptic estimation as a manifold parameter estimation problem. Specifically, a hybrid inference strategy (haptic SLAM) is employed in which discrete object shapes are classified via particle filtering, while the continuous object pose is estimated using analytical gradients for efficient optimization. By continuously updating the parameters of the manipulation potential, the framework dynamically reshapes the induced EM to guide online trajectory replanning and implement uncertainty-aware impedance control, thereby closing the perception-action loop. The system is validated through simulation and over 260 real-world screw-loosening trials. Experimental results demonstrate robust identification and manipulation success in standard scenarios while maintaining accurate tracking. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm that haptic SLAM and uncertainty-aware stiffness modulation outperform fixed impedance baselines, effectively preventing jamming during tight tolerance interactions.

</details>


### [334] [Few-Shot Adaptation to Non-Stationary Environments via Latent Trend Embedding for Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10373)
*Yasuyuki Fujii,Emika Kameda,Hiroki Fukada,Yoshiki Mori,Tadashi Matsuo,Nobutaka Shimada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Trend ID-based adaptation framework for robotic systems facing concept shifts, focusing on estimating latent environmental states rather than altering model parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptation in robotic systems to concept shifts often involves updating model parameters, which can lead to catastrophic forgetting and high computation costs. A scalable alternative is needed.

Method: The method estimates latent environmental states, called Trend IDs, through backpropagation, while keeping model parameters fixed. Temporal regularization and state transition models ensure smooth evolution in the latent space.

Result: Experiments on a food grasping task show Trend IDs maintain distinct latent distributions with consistent trajectories. Few-shot adaptation to unseen environments is achieved without altering model parameters.

Conclusion: The framework offers a computationally efficient, scalable, and interpretable solution for adaptation in robotics across diverse and changing environments.

Abstract: Robotic systems operating in real-world environments often suffer from concept shift, where the input-output relationship changes due to latent environmental factors that are not directly observable. Conventional adaptation methods update model parameters, which may cause catastrophic forgetting and incur high computational cost. This paper proposes a latent Trend ID-based framework for few-shot adaptation in non-stationary environments. Instead of modifying model weights, a low-dimensional environmental state, referred to as the Trend ID, is estimated via backpropagation while the model parameters remain fixed. To prevent overfitting caused by per-sample latent variables, we introduce temporal regularization and a state transition model that enforces smooth evolution of the latent space. Experiments on a quantitative food grasping task demonstrate that the learned Trend IDs are distributed across distinct regions of the latent space with temporally consistent trajectories, and that few-shot adaptation to unseen environments is achieved without modifying model parameters. The proposed framework provides a scalable and interpretable solution for robotics applications operating across diverse and evolving environments.

</details>


### [335] [ScanDP: Generalizable 3D Scanning with Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10390)
*Itsuki Hirako,Ryo Hakoda,Yubin Liu,Matthew Hwang,Yoshihiro Sato,Takeshi Oishi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data-efficient 3D scanning framework is proposed using Diffusion Policy for effective imitation of human-like scanning strategies, enhanced with robust Occupancy Grid Mapping and a hybrid approach for path optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in reinforcement learning-based 3D scanning methods like large data requirements and poor generalization to unseen object categories.

Method: The approach uses Diffusion Policy for human-like scanning, combines Occupancy Grid Mapping (for noise resilience and geometry handling), and incorporates a hybrid sphere-based representation with path optimization to ensure efficiency and robustness.

Result: The method demonstrates higher coverage, shorter paths, and robustness to sensor noise on diverse unseen objects in both shape and scale, outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: The framework ensures practical feasibility, efficient scanning, and strong generalization across diverse objects in real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Learning-based 3D Scanning plays a crucial role in enabling efficient and accurate scanning of target objects. However, recent reinforcement learning-based methods often require large-scale training data and still struggle to generalize to unseen object categories.In this work, we propose a data-efficient 3D scanning framework that uses Diffusion Policy to imitate human-like scanning strategies. To enhance robustness and generalization, we adopt the Occupancy Grid Mapping instead of direct point cloud processing, offering improved noise resilience and handling of diverse object geometries. We also introduce a hybrid approach combining a sphere-based space representation with a path optimization procedure that ensures path safety and scanning efficiency. This approach addresses limitations in conventional imitation learning, such as redundant or unpredictable behavior. We evaluate our method on diverse unseen objects in both shape and scale. Ours achieves higher coverage and shorter paths than baselines, while remaining robust to sensor noise. We further confirm practical feasibility and stable operation in real-world execution.

</details>


### [336] [Safe Probabilistic Planning for Human-Robot Interaction using Conformal Risk Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10392)
*Jake Gonzales,Kazuki Mizuta,Karen Leung,Lillian J. Ratliff*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new probabilistic safe control framework for human-robot interaction, combining control barrier functions with conformal risk control, achieving lower collisions and safety violations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address safety challenges in human-robot interactions caused by complex human behavior and prediction uncertainties.

Method: The method integrates conformal risk control with control barrier functions to ensure safety by dynamically adjusting safety margins based on interaction context.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate reduced collision rates and safety violations, with high success rates in goal-reaching tasks compared to baseline approaches.
